learningGoal: How to tell the difference between acute (less than 90°), right, obtuse (between 90° and 180°), straight, and reflex (more than 180°) angles
Why the size of an angle matters, not the length of the lines

screens.title: What type of angle is that?

interaction.sentence: An angle between 90° and 180° is called ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! An angle between 90° and 180° is obtuse — wider than a right angle (an angle of exactly 90 degrees) but less than a straight line. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — an angle between 90° and 180° is obtuse. Acute is less than 90°, reflex is more than 180°.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Ella found an angle of **35°**. What type of angle is it?

interaction.question: An angle of 35° is...
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 35° is **acute** because it's less than 90°. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 35° is **acute** because it's less than 90°. Remember: acute < 90°, right = 90°, obtuse is 91°-179°, straight = 180°, reflex > 180°.

learningGoal: How to classify any angle by comparing it to 90° and 180°
Why reflex angles (angles bigger than 180°) are the ones people forget most often

screens.title: Quick — what type?
screens.body: Tom is labelling angles on a treasure map for a class project. One angle measures **72°**. Can you name its type in under 5 seconds? The trick is knowing just **two key numbers**...

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Tom found another angle: **155°**. What type is it?

interaction.question: What type of angle is 155°?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 155° is **obtuse**. You nailed it! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 155° is **obtuse**. Check where it sits on the number line — is it before 90°, between 90° and 180°, or after 180°?

learningGoal: How to find a missing angle on a straight line
Why angles on a straight line always add up to 180°

screens.title: What's the missing angle?
screens.body: Grace is measuring angles where two paths meet at a crossroads. One angle is **65°** and the other sits on the same **straight line**. What could the missing angle be?

screens.title: Angles on a straight line add up to 180°

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Spot the line, note the known angle, subtract from 180°. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first spot it's a straight line, then note the known angle, then subtract from 180°.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Grace is measuring angles where two paths meet at a crossroads. One angle is **72°**. What is the other angle on the straight line?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Angles on a straight line add up to 180°. So the missing angle is 180° − 72° = **108°**.

learningGoal: How to avoid confusing 180° with 360° on straight line questions
Why checking your answer matters

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body: Charlie is finding the missing angle on a straight line where one angle is 75°. Charlie wrote: "360° − 75° = **285°**". Something's gone wrong. Can you see it?

steps.why: Does this look right to you?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The angle on a straight line is **83°**. What is the missing angle?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle on the straight line?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! **180° − 83° = 97°**. You used the right rule! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Remember: straight line = 180°. So 180° − 83° = **97°**. Don't use 360° here!

learningGoal: How to find a missing angle around a point
Why angles around a point always add up to 360°

screens.title: Find the missing angle!
screens.body: Sophie is measuring angles where four roads meet at a roundabout. The known angles are **90°, 120°, 80°**. There's one angle missing — and there's a simple rule to find it!

screens.title: Angles around a point add up to 360°
Your turn!
screens.body: Sophie is measuring angles where four roads meet at a roundabout. The known angles are **105°, 95°, 85°**. What is the missing angle?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Add the known angles: 105° + 95° + 85° = 285°. Then subtract from 360°: 360° − 285° = **75°**.

learningGoal: How to avoid using 180° when you need 360°
When to use the 'around a point' rule versus the 'straight line' rule

screens.title: Spot the mistake!
screens.body:
Aisha is finding the missing angle at a point where angles are 140°, 95°, and ?°. Aisha wrote: "180° − 235° = can't do it!"
They're stuck — the number went negative! What went wrong?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The angles around a point are **130°, 100°** and **?°**. What is the missing angle?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle around the point?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **360° − 230° = 130°**. You used the right rule! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! These angles are around a POINT, so use 360°. 360° − 230° = **130°**.

screens.title: Straight line or around a point?

learningGoal: How to find a missing angle in a quadrilateral (a four-sided shape)
Why the four angles in any quadrilateral add up to 360°

screens.title: Find the missing angle in the quadrilateral!
screens.body: Tom is measuring angles on a rectangular picture frame that got squashed. Three angles are **90°**, **110°**, and **80°**. What's the fourth angle?

screens.title: Angles in a quadrilateral add up to 360°

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Add the known angles, recall the 360° total, then subtract. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first add the known angles, then remember they total 360°, then subtract.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Three angles in the quadrilateral are **85°**, **105°**, and **95°**. What is the fourth angle?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Add the three angles: 85° + 105° + 95° = 285°. Then 360° − 285° = **75°**.

learningGoal: How to avoid using 180° for quadrilaterals (four-sided shapes)
Why a quadrilateral is not the same as a triangle

screens.title: What went wrong?
screens.body:
Oscar is finding the fourth angle of a quadrilateral with angles 85°, 100°, and 90°. They wrote: "180° − 275° = impossible!"
The answer came out negative. Can you spot the mistake?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Three angles in a quadrilateral are **95°**, **80°**, and **110°**. What is the fourth?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Quadrilateral = 360°. So 360° − 285° = **75°**.

learningGoal: How to spot an isosceles triangle (a triangle with two equal sides) from the tick marks
Why two equal sides means two equal angles

screens.title: Two sides the same — what does that mean?
screens.body: Daisy is building a symmetrical arch for a school play set. The triangle has **two equal sides** (shown by tick marks). The top angle is **40°**. Can you guess the two base angles without measuring?

interaction.sentence: In an isosceles triangle, two equal sides means two ____ angles
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Equal sides always means equal angles opposite them. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — in an isosceles triangle, the two sides that are equal have equal angles opposite them.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: An isosceles triangle has a top angle of **60°**. What is each base angle?

interaction.question: What is each base angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Subtract the top angle from 180°: 180° − 60° = 120°. Then divide by 2: 120° ÷ 2 = **60°**.

learningGoal: How to avoid forgetting to divide by 2 in isosceles (two equal sides) problems
Why you must identify which angle is the 'odd one out'

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body:
Ben found an isosceles triangle with a top angle of 50°. They wrote: "180° − 50° = 130°, so each base angle is 130°."
But that can't be right — can you see why?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: An isosceles (two equal sides) triangle has a top angle of **70°**. What is each base angle?

interaction.question: What is each base angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Remember to divide by 2! 180° − 70° = 110°, then 110° ÷ 2 = **55°**.

subConcepts.name: Missing Angle in a Right-Angled Triangle

learningGoal: How to use the right angle (90°) as a known angle
Why the other two angles in a right-angled triangle add up to 90°

screens.title: A right angle (an angle of exactly 90 degrees) gives you a head start!
screens.body: Ella is working out angles on a ramp for a school wheelchair access project. The triangle has a **right angle (90°)** and another angle of **35°**. Finding the third angle is easier than you think!

screens.title: The other two angles add up to 90°!
Your turn!
screens.body: A right-angled triangle has one angle of **28°**. What is the missing angle?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! In a right-angled triangle, the other two angles add to 90°. So 90° − 28° = **62°**.

learningGoal: How to spot right-angled triangles in real life
When to use the 90° shortcut

screens.title: Right angles are everywhere!
screens.body: Finn noticed a right-angled triangle in the roof of a bus shelter. Right-angled triangles are one of the **most common shapes** in the real world. Can you guess why architects and builders love them?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A right-angled triangle has another angle of **38°**. What's the third angle?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The other two angles add to 90°. So 90° − 38° = **52°**.

learningGoal: How to set up an equation when angles are given as expressions like 2x or 3x + 10
Why you must substitute back to find the actual angle, not just x

screens.title: Algebra meets angles!
screens.body: Aisha is solving a triangle problem where the angles are x°, 2x°, and 3x°. The angles use **x** instead of numbers. Don't worry — if you know the total, you can find x!

screens.title: Step by step: solve for x, then find each angle
Your turn!
screens.body: The angles in a triangle are **2x°, 3x°, 4x°**. What is the value of **x**?

interaction.question: What is the value of x?

learningGoal: How to avoid giving x as the answer when the question asks for the angle
Why substituting back into the expression is essential

screens.title: So close — but not quite!
screens.body: Ravi solved 2x + 3x + x = 180 and got x = 30. The question asks: "What is the angle 2x°?" Ravi wrote: "**30°**". Is that right?

steps.why: The equation is set up correctly.
But wait — is this the angle they asked for?

screens.title: x is not always the answer!
Your turn!
screens.body: If x = 25, what is the angle **3x°**?

interaction.question: What is the angle 3x°?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! x = 25, so 3x = **75°**. Don't confuse x with the angle!

learningGoal: How to see that the exterior angle (the angle outside the shape) equals the two opposite interior angles (inside angles)
Why this shortcut works every time

screens.title: A magic shortcut for exterior angles!
screens.body: Sophie is investigating angles on a triangular road sign. When you extend one side of a triangle, the **exterior angle** that forms has a cool property. It equals the sum of the **two opposite interior angles**!

screens.title: Exterior angle = sum of two opposite interior angles

interaction.sentence: The exterior angle of a triangle equals the ____ of the two opposite interior angles
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! The exterior angle equals the sum of the two opposite interior angles. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — the exterior angle equals the sum (total) of the two opposite interior angles added together.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The two opposite interior angles are **55°** and **75°**. What is the exterior angle?

interaction.question: What is the exterior angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The exterior angle equals the sum of the two opposite interior angles: 55° + 75° = **130°**.

learningGoal: When to use the exterior angle (outside angle) rule versus the straight line rule
How to identify which interior angles (inside angles) are 'opposite'

screens.title: Quick fact: exterior angles!
screens.body: Ben knows two interior angles of a triangle are 40° and 75°. There's a quick way to find the exterior angle without finding the third interior angle first. Just **add the two opposite interior angles**!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Two interior angles are **45°** and **60°**. What is the exterior angle at the third corner?

interaction.question: What is the exterior angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Add the two opposite interior angles: 45° + 60° = **105°**.

learningGoal: How to spot corresponding angles (in matching positions) and alternate angles (in Z-shapes) with parallel lines (lines that never meet)
Why these angles are equal when lines are parallel

screens.title: Parallel lines create equal angles!
screens.body: Grace is looking at the angles formed where a path crosses two parallel fences. When a line crosses two **parallel lines** (lines that run in the same direction and never meet), some angles are **exactly equal** and some add to 180°. Let's find the pattern!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: One angle is **58°**. What is the **co-interior angle** (the one on the same side, between the parallel lines)?

interaction.question: What is the co-interior angle?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Co-interior angles add to 180°, so 180° − 58° = **122°**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Co-interior angles add to 180°. So 180° − 58° = **122°**.

screens.title: F, Z, and C — the parallel line letters!
screens.body: When you see parallel lines (shown with arrows), look for these letter shapes:

learningGoal: How to quickly identify which angle rule to use with parallel lines (lines that never meet)
When angles are equal versus when they add to 180°

screens.title: Equal or add to 180°?
screens.body: Isaac sees an angle of 48° and needs the alternate angle. The **alternate** angle (the angle on the opposite side of the transversal) — is it **equal** to 48°, or does it **add** to 180°? There's an easy way to remember!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The given angle is **62°**. What is the **alternate** angle?

interaction.question: What is the alternate angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! alternate angles equal. The answer is **62°**.

screens.title: The parallel lines cheat sheet!
screens.body: Just remember which angle pairs are **equal** and which **add to 180°**:

learningGoal: How to calculate the sum of interior angles (inside angles) using (n - 2) x 180°
How to find one angle in a regular polygon (a shape where all sides and angles are equal)

screens.title: What's each angle in a regular pentagon?
screens.body: Priya is calculating angles in a regular pentagon for a tiling project. A pentagon has **5 sides**. In a **regular** polygon (a shape where all sides and angles are equal), all angles are equal — but what are they?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A regular hexagon has 6 sides. What is each interior angle?

learningGoal: How to see that the number of triangles grows as the polygon (multi-sided shape) gets more sides
Why the rule (n - 2) x 180° always works

screens.title: Spot the pattern!
screens.body: Oscar is comparing angles in different regular polygons. Look at how the angle sum grows as the polygon (multi-sided shape) gets more sides. Can you see the pattern?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is each interior angle of a regular **pentagon**?

interaction.question: Each angle of a regular pentagon is...
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Each angle of a regular pentagon is **108°**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Use the rule (n − 2) × 180° and divide by n to find **108°**.

subConcepts.name: Finding a Missing Angle in an Irregular Polygon

learningGoal: How to find the sum of interior angles (inside angles) using (n - 2) × 180°
How to subtract the known angles to find a missing angle

screens.title: What's the missing angle in this pentagon?
screens.body: Ella is calculating a missing angle in an irregular pentagon on her homework. The pentagon has **5 sides** and the known angles are **110°, 95°, 130°, 85°**. One angle is missing!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Find the total, add the known angles, then subtract. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first find the total using (n − 2) × 180°, then add the known angles, then subtract.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: An irregular pentagon has angles of **105°, 100°, 120°, 90°** and one unknown. What is the missing angle?

interaction.question: What is the missing angle?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Total = 540°. Known = 415°. So missing = 540° − 415° = **125°**.

learningGoal: How to spot common mistakes when finding missing angles in polygons (multi-sided shapes)
Why getting the angle sum right is the most important first step

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body: Tom made a mistake finding the missing angle of an irregular pentagon. He got **130°** but the teacher marked it wrong. What went wrong?

screens.title: Now you try!
screens.body: An irregular pentagon has angles **115°, 105°, 90°, 125°** and one unknown. What is it?
